retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Testis-sparing surgery is not a good choice for patients who are not likely to be compliant with mandatory follow-up, especially for small testicular malignancies. This procedure should optimally be performed in high-volume centers where experienced genitourinary pathologists are available to perform the critical frozen section examinations. Testis-sparing surgery should not be performed without reliable frozen section pathological support. [32] There are no specific contraindications to these surgeries except for an active infection, uncorrected coagulation disorders, and anesthesia considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Indications -- Indications for Testis-Sparing Surgery (Partial Orchiectomy)
retrieved_docs.content: High-quality intraoperative frozen section evaluations, by an experienced genitourinary pathologist, should be available.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Despite clear and growing indications as well as recommendations in published American Urological Association and European Association of Urology guidelines, testis-sparing surgery is still performed infrequently. Factors limiting its widespread use include inadequate training and experience with the technique among most practicing urologists, lack of confidence in intraoperative frozen section analysis, unavailability of an experienced and trusted genitourinary pathologist, a recommendation that these complex procedures be performed in high-volume tertiary care centers of excellence, and the requirement for intraoperative ultrasonography and surgical microscopy skills for impalpable lesions. [4] [7] [223] [229]

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Testis-sparing surgery is a reasonable option for males with small, solid testicular neoplasms, as frozen section procedure is highly reliable in detecting malignancies intraoperatively, conversion to a radical orchiectomy does not appear to negatively affect oncological outcomes, and appropriate adjuvant treatment (radiotherapy) provides excellent control if germ cell CIS is detected in the residual parenchymal biopsies. [195] [206]

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Technique or Treatment -- Testis-Sparing Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: Testis-sparing surgery can be challenging, especially for impalpable neoplasms. The goal is to remove all neoplastic tissue while preserving as much healthy testicular parenchyma as possible. [4] [7] The remaining testis tissue needs to function normally without atrophy or fibrosis. [7] [32] Achieving this requires skill, experience, careful planning, ideally the use of magnification loupes or a surgical microscope, and possibly intraoperative ultrasonography for impalpable lesions. [4] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients being considered for testis-sparing surgery should be thoroughly counseled about the risk of residual disease or positive margins, the limitation of frozen section examinations, conversion to a radical orchiectomy if the frozen section is equivocal or suspicious, the possible need for radiation therapy if CIS is discovered, and the necessity for long-term monitoring and follow-up examinations. [1] [2] [7] [29] [126] [127] [169]

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: Radical orchidectomy requires a urological surgeon, at least an assistant, a scrub nurse or technician, and an anesthetist to administer the anesthesia. As with other open surgical procedures, the standard operating room support staff is also needed. Testis-sparing surgery requires the availability of intraoperative frozen section procedure and an experienced genitourinary pathologist.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Indications -- Pediatric Testicular Tumors and Surgical Approach
retrieved_docs.content: Young post-pubertal male individuals are substantially more likely to have a malignancy, so testis-sparing surgery is not routinely indicated, and they typically undergo a radical orchiectomy, which remains the gold standard. [155] However, patients with localized testicular neoplasms with negative tumor markers and imaging may be considered for testis-sparing surgery, as the majority of these lesions are benign, and the effects of an orchiectomy in such young patients should be considered. If testis-sparing surgery is performed, an intraoperative frozen section analysis is mandatory, along with long-term follow-up to monitor growth, sexual development, late recurrences, and psychological health. [150] [154] [161]

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Technique or Treatment -- Testis-Sparing Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: Frozen section: The neoplasm, additional biopsies from the tumor bed, and the marginal tissue should be sent for frozen section examination to identify any suspicious histology, CIS, and involvement of the surgical margins. [4] [7] [213] Fortunately, intraoperative frozen section analysis is highly reliable, with a positive predictive value of 98% to 100% and <1% of malignant tumors being initially misread as benign. [4] [33] [217] [218]

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Indications -- Pediatric Testicular Tumors and Surgical Approach
retrieved_docs.content: Unnecessary orchiectomies can be prevented in the pediatric age group with proper evaluation and management of testicular neoplasms. [150] Preoperative scrotal ultrasounds with selective color Doppler, contrast-enhanced ultrasonography, elastography, and MRI, along with serum tumor markers and intraoperative frozen sections, allow testis-sparing surgical procedures to be safely performed for smaller lesions, with adequate safety margins and the maximum preservation of testicular parenchyma to maintain hormonal levels and postoperative spermatogenesis. [154] [158] [162] [163] [164] The majority of solid testicular tumors in post-pubertal males are malignant; therefore, radical orchiectomy remains the standard of care, although testis-sparing surgery with intraoperative frozen sections may be considered in select cases. [150]

retrieved_docs.title: Germ Cell Seminoma -- Evaluation -- Biopsy
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical biopsy is indicated for histological confirmation.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Technique or Treatment -- Testis-Sparing Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: Removing the neoplasm: The lesion is typically separated easily from the surrounding testicular tissue by enucleation due to the frequent presence of a pseudocapsule. [4] [7] [150] [212] If this is not possible, it may be sharply excised. [150] Difficulty in extracting the neoplasm suggests a malignancy with possible involvement of adjacent structures. [7] [213] Multiple biopsies (at least 4) from the surrounding normal testicular tissue should also be taken to identify any in situ germ cell neoplasia or CIS. [1] [2] [29] [212] [213] [214] In addition to enucleation or resection of the primary neoplasm, a 2 to 5 mm tissue margin surrounding the lesion should also be resected, scraped away, or removed. [4] [153] A biopsy of the tumor bed is also recommended. [215] [216]

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Indications -- Guidelines and Considerations for Testis-Sparing Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should be aware that radiation therapy may be required if biopsies of the residual testicular parenchyma show the presence of carcinoma in situ (CIS) (reported in 84% of such patients), which progresses to invasive disease without further treatment. [29] Radiation therapy should be performed if the histology from the biopsies shows evidence of CIS in the residual testicular parenchyma. However, adjunctive radiation therapy is recommended after testis-sparing surgery for germ cell malignancies regardless of the biopsy results. [29] [150] [151] [152]

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Orchiectomy and Testis-Sparing Surgery for Testicular Neoplasms -- Technique or Treatment -- Testis-Sparing Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: If the frozen section histology indicates a benign lesion with negative margins, the testis is salvageable and can be safely returned to the scrotum. [2] [146] A repeat examination of the testis using an ultrasound or microscopy is recommended before closure of the tunica to ensure that all neoplastic tissue has been successfully removed. [199] Bleeding should be controlled through meticulous hemostasis using cautery; the incision in the testis should be irrigated, the defect in the tunica albuginea closed with a small running absorbable suture, the tourniquet released (if present), and the testicle carefully replaced in the scrotum, taking care to avoid twisting of the cord. [4] [7] [219] The wound is then repaired in the same manner as the radical orchiectomy procedure described previously.

retrieved_docs.title: Azoospermia -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic Testicular Biopsy
retrieved_docs.content: The design of the testicular tissue is heterogeneous, and spermatogenesis occurs in the foci; hence, a biopsy is infrequently utilized as a diagnostic tool. [63] As a rule, testicular characteristics and diagnostic lab findings are good but imperfect indicators of NOA. Hence, testicular sperm extraction (TESE) can be performed in a specialized assisted reproduction center, which permits cryopreservation of sperm and avoids testicular biopsy. A normal testicular biopsy result is suggestive of obstruction, and vasography is required to identify the precise location of the blockage. A formal surgical repair can then be performed or sperm retrieval as appropriate. Depending on the severity of the defect, serum testosterone levels can be low, normal, or high. Only a single testis needs to be biopsied for a histological diagnosis. The larger of the 2 testes should be selected for the procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Leydig Cell Cancer of the Testis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: While primary tumor biopsy before orchiectomy is contraindicated, an intraoperative frozen section can guide management. Frozen section evaluation may be considered when there are no signs of malignancy on imaging, the neoplasm is solitary, bilateral testicular tumors are present, the mass is small (<2 cm), and serum tumor marker results are negative. This approach avoids unnecessary orchiectomies in selected patients willing to undergo continued surveillance. [119] [120] [145] [155] [156] The intraoperative decision regarding testis-sparing surgery is based on mandatory frozen section examination of the tumor; evidence of malignancy requires radical orchiectomy. [119] [120] [157]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 539
retrieved_docs.content: Biospecimen Collection Method is defined by the value “الخزعة” (biopsy). The biopsy procedure functions as a method within both diagnostic endocrine procedures and diagnostic procedures; male genital. Within the hierarchy of specimen‑collection techniques, “الخزعة” has a child entity “لطاخة المهبل” (vaginal swab), indicating that vaginal swab is a specific subtype of biopsy‑related collection. Additionally, Transrectal biopsy of prostate is a child of diagnostic procedures; male genital, representing a specialized application of biopsy for prostate evaluation. Together, these relationships illustrate how biopsy serves as a central biospecimen collection method across endocrine and male genital diagnostics, with distinct sub‑procedures such as vaginal swab and transrectal prostate biopsy extending its clinical relevance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2188
retrieved_docs.content: Disorder of male reproductive system is a broad category of conditions affecting the male reproductive organs, and within this category سرطان الخصية (testicular cancer) is a specific malignant disorder of the testes, illustrating that testicular cancer is a subset of male reproductive system disorders and highlighting its relevance in male reproductive health and oncology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2319
retrieved_docs.content: Biospecimen Collection Method includes the value “الخزعة” (biopsy). Diagnostic endocrine procedures have a broader relationship with الخزعة, indicating that biopsy can be employed within endocrine diagnostic contexts. The category diagnostic procedures; male genital lists الخزعة as a method, showing that biopsy is a technique used to evaluate male genital conditions. Transrectal biopsy of prostate is connected to عمليات البروستاتا (prostate procedures) through a broader‑relationship link, and it is also a child entity of diagnostic procedures; male genital, establishing it as a specialized male genital diagnostic procedure. Together, these relationships illustrate that الخزعة is a fundamental biospecimen collection method across multiple diagnostic domains—including endocrine and male genital assessments—and that the Transrectal biopsy of prostate exemplifies its specific application within prostate‑related procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1835
retrieved_docs.content: Transrectal biopsy of prostate is classified as a diagnostic procedure within the male genital system, indicating its role in evaluating prostate pathology in men. This procedure is organized under the broader category of male genital diagnostic techniques and is noted to have subordinate or related child procedures that further refine its clinical application.

These procedures are grouped under “إجراءات هيستوباثولوجيا متعلقة بالأعضاء التناسلية والثدي” and the child category “Other diagnostic procedures; female organs,” which itself is a child of عملية للأعضاء التناسلية الأنثوية (female genital‑organ procedures). خزرعة بطانة الرحم (endometrial biopsy) is the target‑anatomy for both open and diagnostic dilation‑and‑curettage (D&C) of the uterus, illustrating a common diagnostic pathway for uterine pathology. * Aspiration curettage after delivery or abortion, classified as “Dilatation and curettage (D&C); aspiration after delivery or abortion,” also employs الخزعة for tissue retrieval. * Male genital diagnostics, specifically transrectal prostate biopsy, is a child of عمليات البروستاتا (prostate operations) and belongs to “diagnostic procedures; male genital,” highlighting its role in prostate pathology assessment. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2611
retrieved_docs.content: Transrectal biopsy of prostate is a specific diagnostic procedure performed on the male genital system that falls under the broader category of prostate operations (عمليات البروستاتا). As a child technique within the group of male genital diagnostic procedures, the transrectal approach is used to obtain tissue samples from the prostate for pathological evaluation, thereby linking it directly to the larger framework of prostate‑related surgical interventions. This relationship highlights how the transrectal biopsy serves both as a focused diagnostic tool and as part of the comprehensive set of procedures encompassed by prostate operations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2598
retrieved_docs.content: Fine needle biopsy of the ovary is a specific application of the fine needle biopsy technique (خزعة بإبرة رفيعة), utilizing a thin needle to obtain tissue samples from ovarian lesions for diagnostic evaluation. This method allows minimally invasive access to ovarian tissue, facilitating accurate histopathological analysis while reducing procedural morbidity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: female‑organ diagnostics comprise vaginal smear (لطاخة المهبل), endocervical biopsy, open uterine biopsy, pelvic endoscopy (تنظير جوف الحوض), vaginal biopsy (خزعة المهبل), hysteroscopy (منظار الرحم), diagnostic dilation and curettage of the uterus, and fine‑needle ovarian biopsy (خزعة بإبرة رفيعة), all performed by Physicians – Obstetrics & Gynecology. Male genital diagnostics include transrectal prostate biopsy, a child of “diagnostic procedures; male genital,” and transurethral prostate incision (شق البروستاتا عبر الإحليل). Procedures that are explicitly separated for coding purposes include irrigation of an implanted venous access device, which must not be coded simultaneously with ovarian biopsy, transurethral prostatectomy, heart‑valve repair, ear‑drum repair, facial tightening, skin graft, phototherapy, platelet apheresis, laparoscopic treatment of ectopic pregnancy without salpingectomy or oophorectomy, kidney transplant, or other listed interventions. Collectively, these diverse techniques illustrate how Exploration procedure encompasses a wide range of operative interventions aimed at diagnostic assessment, therapeutic removal, or biopsy of pathological lesions across multiple anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2476
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an organ encompasses a variety of anatomical structures, including gonads and lymph nodes, reflecting the broad classification of bodily tissues that perform distinct physiological functions. In clinical practice, organs may be the focus of several procedural interventions. A vaginal biopsy (خزعة المهبل) can involve the excision of organ tissue for diagnostic evaluation, while pelvic cavity endoscopy (تنظير جوف الحوض) and skin grafting (طُعم الجلد) target organ structures as part of therapeutic or investigative procedures. Understanding that organs can represent both gonadal and lymphatic components, and recognizing the specific procedures that excise or target these tissues, is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and comprehensive patient care.

This integrated framework organizes genitourinary cancers by organ, anatomical site, and malignant‑tumor grouping, and links them to diagnostic coding, surgical management, and screening strategies essential for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 138
retrieved_docs.content: tumor and to the vomer from other primary sites, each retaining the classifications of both “ورم خبيث” and “ورم خبيث أولي”. Female breast cancer management includes diagnostic interventions (breast biopsy, other diagnostic procedures) and surgical treatments such as lumpectomy and quadrantectomy. The male reproductive system hierarchy places testicular cancer (سرطان الخصية) as a primary disorder, while penile cancer (سرطان قضيبي) represents malignancy of other male genital organs. Overall, the knowledge base interconnects organ‑specific primary and metastatic malignant neoplasms, their morphological classifications, anatomical finding sites (areola, nipple, seminal vesicles, vomer, nasal bone, thymus, etc.), and related clinical procedures, providing a unified framework for recognizing, classifying, and managing cancers across breast, genitourinary, head‑and‑neck, and other systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2529
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures; male genital refer to examinations and interventions performed on male genital organs, and the prostate (prostata) is a partially excised anatomy that is commonly involved in such diagnostic procedures. The prostate (prostata) is also the anatomic site associated with metastatic prostate cancer (سرطان البروستاتا النَّقيلي). Recognizing that the prostate can be partially removed for diagnostic evaluation and that it serves as the primary location for this malignant disease is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and appropriate patient care.

Collectively, the integrated knowledge of urogenital anatomy, renal and prostate pathology, the full spectrum of female reproductive disorders, laparoscopic and peritoneal surgical techniques, extracorporeal lithotripsy, sexual dysfunction categories, and targeted pharmacologic therapies illustrates a comprehensive framework for patient assessment and multidisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 643
retrieved_docs.content: Biospecimen Collection Method is linked to the concept الخزعة (biopsy) as a specific value, indicating that biopsy is a primary technique for obtaining tissue samples in clinical practice. The term الخزعة is also classified within the Medical & Physical Treatment (PsycINFO Subcluster Term), showing that it is considered a therapeutic and diagnostic intervention in the broader context of medical and physical treatments. Diagnostic endocrine procedures have a broader relationship with الخزعة, reflecting that biopsy is employed in endocrine system evaluations to obtain specimens for hormonal and pathological analysis. In addition, الخزعة serves as the method for diagnostic procedures; male genital and for other diagnostic procedures of the urinary tract, underscoring its role as a fundamental technique across male genital and urinary system investigations. Within the hierarchy of procedures, الخزعة includes لطاخة المهبل (vaginal swab) as a child concept, illustrating that vaginal swabbing is a specialized form of biopsy targeting the vaginal mucosa. Transrectal biopsy of prostate is related to عمليات البروستاتا (prostate procedures) through a broader relationship, positioning this specific biopsy as a key component of prostate-related surgical and diagnostic interventions. Moreover, Transrectal biopsy of prostate is identified as a child of diagnostic procedures; male genital, further linking it to the male genital diagnostic framework and confirming its status as a specialized male genital biopsy technique. Together, these relationships depict biopsy (الخزعة) as a central, versatile diagnostic method that spans endocrine, male genital, urinary, and vaginal applications, and integrates into both general biospecimen collection practices and specific procedural categories such as prostate operations.
